recent.question: Will the highest temperature in New York City be between 78-79°F on June 22?

open_bets.question: Will the highest temperature in New York City be between 78-79°F on June 22?
Will the highest temperature in Miami be between 94-95°F on June 22?
Will the highest temperature in Seattle be between 86-87°F on June 22?
Will the highest temperature in Miami be between 94-95°F on June 21?

lessons.root_cause: The bet relied on global ensemble forecasts (GFS and ECMWF) to predict a highly precise 2-degree temperature bucket in San Francisco during June, failing to account for the models' systematic inability to resolve localized marine layer cooling (June Gloom), which resulted in a major over-prediction.
lessons.rule: Do not trade narrow temperature buckets in coastal microclimates during peak marine-layer seasons using only global ensembles, as these models frequently fail to capture localized onshore wind and fog cooling.

lessons.root_cause: Chasing a nominal edge (6.0% vs 0.8% market implied) on a highly specific tail-risk bucket (70-71°F) that was more than 5°F below the consensus of both the GFS (78.2°F) and ECMWF (76.6°F) ensemble means.
lessons.rule: Avoid betting on narrow, extreme tail buckets that deviate significantly from the consensus model means, as the absolute probability of success remains extremely low regardless of the nominal calculated edge.

lessons.root_cause: The ensemble probability model overestimated the likelihood of an extreme tail-event temperature (96-97°F, which is 5.4-6.4°F above the ensemble mean of 90.6°F) in a coastal tropical city (Miami) where maritime moderation and sea breezes typically cap maximum temperatures, creating a false edge.
lessons.rule: For coastal or tropical locations, heavily discount model-derived tail probabilities for extreme high-temperature buckets, as standard ensemble variance fails to account for local physical capping mechanisms like sea breezes and convective cloud cover.

lessons.root_cause: Bought an extreme cold-tail contract (<= 87°F) based on an apparent statistical edge (5.1% ensemble probability vs 0.1% market price) when both GFS and ECMWF means strongly agreed on a hot day (> 91.5°F), ignoring that raw ensemble members often over-disperse and create artificial tail probability in the absence of a real physical cooling mechanism.
lessons.rule: Do not buy ultra-cheap tail contracts based solely on nominal ensemble probability edge unless there is a physical, synoptic driver (such as a cold front or precipitation) to support the model deviation.

lessons.root_cause: Betting on an outcome that the predictive ensemble assigned a zero probability, indicating a complete lack of model support.
lessons.rule: Never place a bet on an outcome when your primary predictive model ensemble assigns a 0% probability to it.

principles.claim: Do not buy ultra-cheap tail contracts based solely on nominal ensemble probability edge unless there is a physical, synoptic driver (such as a cold front or precipitation) to support the model deviation.
